coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent and smartphone-assisted dual-mode sensor for folic acid determination based on N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs

coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Lin
Liu, Lizhen
Bai, Yunfeng
Wang, Yuzhen
Zhao, Lu
Mi, Zhi
Qin, Jun
Feng, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Accurate detection of folic acid (FA) content in foods is crucial for guiding dietary supplementation and mitigating risks associated with deficiency or overdose. However, most current methods have limitations in on-site visual detection. This study addresses this gap by developing a dual-mode ratiometric fluorescent and smartphone-assisted sensor (N-Nb2C QD/CQDs). The internal filter effect between the sensor and FA significantly changed the fluorescence intensity ratio and produced continuous fluorescence color changes. The fluorescence color was converted to RGB values using smartphone, enabling visual and quantitative determination of FA. Under optimal parameters, the linear ranges for detecting FA were found to be 0.1–400 and 0.1–350 μM for fluorescence spectrometer and smartphone-assisted sensor. The detection limits were 87 and 189 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the sensor was successfully applied to direct visual identification of FA in samples of orange juice, oatmeal and urine, indicating the possibility of its practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence intensity ratio
Inner filter effect
Food assays

FA analysis in actual orange juice, oatmeal and urine samples
Ratiometric fluorescent sensor determination of FA
Ratiometric fluorescent sensor determination of FA in real samples
Smartphone-assisted sensor determination of FA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM picture of Nb2AlC. (b) TEM image of F-Nb2C. (c) TEM image of N-Nb2C QDs, inset: the size distribution histogram. (d) HR-TEM lattice fringes of N-Nb2C QDs. (e) EDS spectrum of N-Nb2C QDs. (f) XRD patterns of Nb2AlC and N-Nb2C QDs. (g) FT-IR spectrum of N-Nb2C QDs. (h) XPS Full spectrum of N-Nb2C QDs. (i) N 1 s spectrum of N-Nb2C QDs.
(a) TEM image of CQDs, inset: the size distribution histogram. (b) FT-IR spectrum of CQDs. (c) XPS Full spectrum of CQDs.

(a) FL and absorption spectra of N-Nb2C QDs. (b) Emission spectra at 320–400 nm excitation wavelengths of N-Nb2C QDs. (c) FL and absorption spectra of CQDs. (d) Emission spectra at 370–420 nm excitation wavelengths of CQDs. (e) CIE chromaticity diagram of the N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs.

(a) Emission spectra of N-Nb2C QDs, CQDs, and N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs. (b) Emission spectrum of N-Nb2C QDs and absorption spectrum of CQDs. (c) Absorption spectra of CQDs, N-Nb2C QDs, N-Nb2C QDs + CQDs, and Absorption (N-Nb2C QDs) + Absorption (CQDs). (d) FL lifetimes of N-Nb2C QDs and N-Nb2C QDs + CQDs. (e) I452/I544 of N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs in different Xe irradiation time. (f) I452/I544 of N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs in different concentrations of NaCl solution.

(a) Emission spectra of N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (b) Emission spectra of 250 μL N-Nb2C QDs with different volumes CQDs. (c) The effect of pH on the I452/I544. (d) The effect of incubation time on the I452/I544.
(a) FL spectra of N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs with different concentrations of FA. (b-c) The linear relationships between I452/I544 and concentration of FA.
The effect of selective and anti-interference experiments on I452/I544.

(a) Absorption spectra of FA, N-Nb2C QDs, N-Nb2C QDs + FA, and Abs (N-Nb2C QDs) + Abs (FA). (b) Absorption spectra of FA, CQDs, CQDs + FA, and Abs (CQDs) + Abs (FA). (c) FL spectra of N-Nb2C QDs and absorption spectrum of FA. (d) FL spectra of CQDs and absorption spectrum of FA. (e) The FL lifetimes of N-Nb2C QDs and N-Nb2C QDs + FA. (f) The FL lifetimes of CQDs and CQDs + FA.

(a) Schematic illustration of smartphone-assisted sensor for the recognition of FA. (b) FL images under UV light of the sensor with FA (0.1–350 μM). (c) The linear relationship between FA concentration and B/R. (d) CIE chromaticity diagram of the sensor with FA.
(a) Synthesis of N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs. (b) The N-Nb2C QD/CQDs ratiometric fluorescent and smartphone-assisted sensor for FA detection.
Analytical results of FA in real samples.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent and smartphone-assisted dual-mode sensor for folic acid determination based on N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030002, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030002, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate detection of folic acid (FA) content in foods is crucial for guiding dietary supplementation and mitigating risks associated with deficiency or overdose. However, most current methods have limitations in on-site visual detection. This study addresses this gap by developing a dual-mode ratiometric fluorescent and smartphone-assisted sensor (N-Nb2C QD/CQDs). The internal filter effect between the sensor and FA significantly changed the fluorescence intensity ratio and produced continuous fluorescence color changes. The fluorescence color was converted to RGB values using smartphone, enabling visual and quantitative determination of FA. Under optimal parameters, the linear ranges for detecting FA were found to be 0.1–400 and 0.1–350 μM for fluorescence spectrometer and smartphone-assisted sensor. The detection limits were 87 and 189 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the sensor was successfully applied to direct visual identification of FA in samples of orange juice, oatmeal and urine, indicating the possibility of its practical application.

Scheme 1 (a) Synthesis of N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs. (b) The N-Nb2C QD/CQDs ratiometric fluorescent and smartphone-assisted sensor for FA detection.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The European Prospective Investigation into Cancer and Nutrition (EPIC) recommends a folic acid (FA), intake of 250–350 μg/day for men and 200–300 μg/day for women (Park et al., 2012).

Insufficient or excessive intake of FA can adversely impact physical health (Eichholzer et al., 2006). Chronic diseases such as mental disorders, heart disease, and leukopenia, especially those related to pregnancy abnormalities, are all associated with FA deficiency (Prasad et al., 2010; Zhu et al., 2013). On the other hand, excessive intake of FA can negatively affect the absorption of zinc and cobalamin vitamin (Akbar et al., 2016; World Health Organization, 2012). As diet is one of the main ways humans obtain FA, developing rapid, accurate, and sensitive methods for detecting FA in daily food is crucial for accurately guiding dietary supplementation.

Several methods have been reported for detecting FA, including capillary electrophoresis (Uysal et al., 2010; Zhao et al., 2021), spectrophotometry (Chen et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2023), electrochemical assays (Dai et al., 2016; Silva-Neto et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2023), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (Zhang et al., 2012), high-performance liquid chromatography (Leporati et al., 2005; Wang et al., 2018). Among them, fluorescence (FL) analysis has gained favor with researchers due to its advantages of a straightforward detection process that requires less time while offering high sensitivity (Jia et al., 2023). For ratiometric FL sensors, the inherent self-calibration function can greatly avoid the influence of environmental factors and equipment variability, which can significantly improve the accuracy and sensitivity of detection (Liu et al., 2014). Typically, ratiometric sensors combine the spectral features of two materials without sensitization (Fan et al., 2023). More importantly, the ratiometric sensor is accompanied by obvious color changes, allowing for intuitive identification of analytes through the combination of devices such as smartphones (Wang et al., 2024).

In recent years, numerous quantum dots (QDs) have been developed and applied as fluorescent sensors owing to the inherent strengths of FL methods (Chen et al., 2022; Jiao et al., 2023). In particular, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have the advantages of easy synthesis and strong FL, while Nb2C QDs exhibit good dispersibility and photostability (Guo et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2020). A new ratiometric sensor can be constructed from two QDs by simple mixing without the need for complex chemical assembly. The ratiometric sensor integrates the spectral features of CQDs and Nb2C QDs without sensitization. Due to the richer spectral information and potential for visualization analysis, the prepared sensor is considered an ideal tool for detecting FA in food. To our knowledge, FA sensors that combine ratiometric FL and visualization capabilities have not been reported.

Herein, a N-Nb2C QD/CQDs based smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor was developed for the visualization and quantitative detection of FA (Scheme 1 ). The N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method. N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs were prepared by mixing N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs, exhibiting two emission wavelengths at 452 and 544 nm when excited at 380 nm. In the presence of FA, the two emission peaks of the sensor exhibited differential decreases, and the unique signal response could realize the sensitive detection of FA. Moreover, considering the requirements of on-site quantization in practical applications, a smartphone-based application was interfaced for image acquisition and red-green-blue (RGB) analysis. The significant FL color change from yellow to blue enabled instant visual assessment of FA. Additionally, ‌the feasibility of the dual-mode sensor was validated through spiked recovery methods in real food samples such as orange juice and oatmeal. The constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensor, combined with a smartphone, provided a convenient, fast and visually interpretable method for quantifying FA in food samples.

Forsman Scientific Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China) provided Nb2AlC ceramic powder (90 %, 200 mesh). KCl, NaCl, AlCl3, ZnCl2, CaCl2, BaCl2, MnCl2, PbCl2, arginine, tyrosine, tryptophan, serine, glutamate, phenylalanine, histidine, methionine, alanine, glycine, homocysteine, valine, leucine, threonine, cysteine, ammonia, FA (97 %), and hydrofluoric acid (HF) solution (40.0 %) were purchased from Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Urea, o-phenylenediamine, glucose, maltose, lactose, fructose, vitamin B1, sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4), and ascorbic acid were supplied by Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH) was obtained from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The experimental water was deionized water by a Millipore Milli-Q system (Bedford, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
N-Nb
2
C QDs: N-Nb2C QDs were synthesized based on the reported methods with some modifications (Liu et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2020). The Nb2AlC ceramic powder (2 g) was gradually mixed with HF acid (20 mL) and stirred for 48 h to remove the Al layer. The multilayer Nb2C MXene (M-Nb2C) was acquired by centrifugation (1123 ×g, 5 min) followed by washing 4 times with water. Then, M-Nb2C was dispersed in TPAOH for 72 h. Few-layer Nb2C MXene (F-Nb2C) was collected by high-speed centrifugation (9166 ×g, 10 min) and washed 8 times to neutral. F-Nb2C was homogeneously dispersed in water and sonicated (900 W, 5 h). Subsequently, the suspension was collected by centrifugation (1123 ×g, 1 h). N-Nb2C QDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method. Ammonia was used to dope N in the hydrothermal process. The supernatant (5 mL) was mixed thoroughly with ammonia (2 mL) and water (13 mL). Then, the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave reaction for 10 h at 160 °C. After cooling, the yellow solution underwent dialysis (MWCO: 500 Da) to achieve neutrality and was filtered through a filter membrane (0.22 μm). Finally, the resulting N-Nb2C QDs solution 40 mL was freeze-dried for 5 d and the resulting powder was collected and stored for further work.

CQDs: Typically, urea (0.15 g) and o-Phenylenediamine (0.3 g) were dissolved in water (20 mL) via sonication (900 W, 10 min). The yellow clarified solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined steel stainless autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. The crude product of the reaction was centrifuged (5867 ×g, 5 min) and filtered through a filter membrane (0.22 μm). Ultimately, the 40 mL filter solution was freeze-dried for 5 d and the resulting powder was collected and stored for further work.

The sensor was prepared by simple mixing of N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs. The N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs powders obtained previously were prepared into 20 mg/mL solution with PBS (0.01 M, pH = 7), respectively. After that, 250 μL N-Nb2C QDs solution and 150 μL CQDs solution were taken separately and diluted to 3 mL with PBS. The obtained solution was ultrasonically mixed for 10 min and stored at 4 °C for future use.

FL spectra were recorded after FA standard solution was added to the N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs solution. Under the same conditions, different interfering substances were introduced into the N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs solution to examine selectivity, including K+, Na+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, arginine, tyrosine, tryptophan, serine, glutamate, phenylalanine, histidine, methionine, alanine, glycine, homocysteine, valine, leucine, threonine, cysteine, glucose, maltose, lactose, fructose, vitamin B1, and ascorbic acid (Li et al., 2024). The anti-interference experiment was performed by adding interfering substances to the N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs solution before the addition of FA (Peng et al., 2022). The FL of N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs was recorded separately and the FL intensity ratio (I452/I544) was calculated for comparison. All experiments in the study were measured in parallel 3 times.

The mixed solution of FA with varying concentrations and N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs was prepared and homogenized. Then, the images were captured using a smartphone (Vivo X70) under UV light at 365 nm. Color recognition software (Color Picker 4.2.0) was employed to obtain the corresponding RGB values. Ultimately, a linear relationship was established between the B/R value and the FA concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FA analysis in actual orange juice, oatmeal and urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: Two different forms of food containing FA, liquid and solid, were selected to test the ability of the sensor in practical applications. Orange juice and oatmeal, which are consumed frequently, were chosen as samples in a diet containing FA. Orange juice and oatmeal were purchased from a local supermarket. A total of 0.3 g of oatmeal was suspended in 5 mL PBS, while 1 mL of orange juice was diluted to 5 mL with PBS. Both the oatmeal suspension and the orange juice were centrifuged (13,200 ×g, 10 min) to collect supernatants. Then, supernatants were filtered through a filter membrane (0.22 μm). Urine was taken from a healthy volunteer. 1 mL of urine was diluted to 5 mL with PBS before undergoing the same centrifugation and filtration processes. These samples were stored at 4 °C before being analyzed.

SEM and TEM were utilized to explore the morphology of Nb2AlC, F-Nb2C, and N-Nb2C QDs. It was observed that the bulk Nb2AlC transformed into F-Nb2C following treatment with HF and TPAOH (Fig. 2a-b). Subsequently, F-Nb2C was processed into numerous small spherical N-Nb2C QDs via hydrothermal methods. The particle size distribution for N-Nb2C QDs ranged from 1.75 to 3.37 nm, with an average particle size of 2.4 nm (Fig. 2c). This confirmed that parent bulk Nb2AlC has been converted into zero-dimensional structure Nb2C QDs. HR-TEM was applied to learn the crystallographic features of N-Nb2C QDs. The lattice fringe spacing of 0.22 nm for N-Nb2C QDs was visible, which corresponded to the (101) lattice of the single Nb2C layer (Fig. 2d) (Xu et al., 2020). The TEM-EDS point-scan image showed that key elements present in N-Nb2C QDs included Nb, C, N, and O, which depicted the successful doping of N (Fig. 2e). XRD measurements further elucidated the structural properties of N- Nb2C QDs. It is noteworthy that no peak appeared at around 39°, confirming the successful removal of the Al layer in Nb2AlC (Fig. 2f) (Rakhi et al., 2015). Meanwhile, a broad amorphous peak attributed to the presence of carbon-rich structure in the N-Nb2C QDs emerged at 24.5° (Yan et al., 2021). These findings proved the successful synthesis of N-Nb2C QDs. In addition, various water-soluble functional groups were identified in the FT-IR spectrum of N-Nb2C QDs, such as -NH2 and -OH (Fig. 2g) (Zhao et al., 2019). XPS analysis was utilized to further understand the surface states and chemical compositions of N-Nb2C QDs. The full XPS spectrum revealed five major peaks corresponding to Nb 3d, C 1 s, Nb 3p, N 1 s, and O 1 s (Fig. 2h) (Xu et al., 2020). High-resolution fitting of the N 1 s spectrum gained two peaks at 206.2 and 208.91 eV, which belonged to the Nb(V)-O and Nb-Ox, respectively (Fig. 2i) (Mashtalir et al., 2015). This result further substantiated the successful doping of N into N-Nb2C QDs.

The uniform distribution of CQDs was portrayed in the TEM image (Fig. 3a). Statistical analysis indicated that the diameter of CQDs ranged from 1.96 to 4.15 nm with an average diameter of 2.87 nm. The surface of CQDs contained abundant hydrophilic functional groups, contributing to its excellent water solubility (Fig. 3b) (Ding et al., 2022). The full XPS spectrum of the CQDs displayed three peaks related to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s (Fig. 3c) (Guo et al., 2022). High-resolution XPS spectra analysis results were found to be consistent with the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1). Both XPS and FT-IR analysis showed that the surface of CQDs contained a significant number of hydrophilic groups.

UV–vis and FL spectra were employed to explore the optical properties of N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs. N-Nb2C QDs solution was light yellow in natural light while showing blue FL under UV light. In the UV–vis spectrum, a broad absorption band appeared from 231 to 289 nm, which corresponded to the trapping of the excited state energy by the surface states (Fig. 4a) (Xu et al., 2018). The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths for N-Nb2C QDs were at 360 and 452 nm, respectively. Moreover, excitation-dependent behavior was observed at different excitation wavelengths, which mainly contributed to surface defects and size effects (Fig. 4b) (Lee et al., 2016; Li et al., 2015). The CQDs solution was yellow, transparent, and clear with yellow FL under UV light. There were three obvious absorption peaks in the UV–vis spectrum of the CQDs at 235, 289, and 413 nm, which originated from the π-π* transition of CC, n-π* transition of CO, and trapping surface state, respectively (Fig. 4c) (Wang et al., 2010). Its optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were located at 400 and 544 nm, respectively. CQDs exhibited excitation-independent behavior when the excitation wavelength changed (Fig. 4d). This was caused by the uniform surface states of CQDs (Wen & Yin, 2016). Besides, the corresponding CIE images of the N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs were largely similar to the colors observed by the eye (Fig. 4e). In addition, the absolute QYs of N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs were measured at 3.2 and 9.4 %, respectively.

The N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs were constructed by mixing N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs, with two emission peaks at 452 and 544 nm under the excitation of 380 nm (Fig. 5a). The emission spacing of 92 nm suggested its potential for ratiometric analysis applications. Notably, the FL of N-Nb2C QDs was significantly quenched while the CQDs was enhanced during the mixing process. Then, the mechanism of this phenomenon was explored. The emission spectrum of N-Nb2C QDs overlapped with the absorption spectrum of CQDs, while the UV–vis absorption spectrum did not show any change after the N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs were mixed (Fig. 5b-c). These findings suggested a possible Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) between N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs. Furthermore, the FL lifetime of N-Nb2C QDs changed from 4.75 to 3.44 ns after mixing CQDs, providing additional evidence supporting FRET between N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs (Fig. 5d) (Dewangan et al., 2021). Subsequently, the stability of N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs was examined in both photostability and salt stability. The I452/I544 did not change significantly during exposure to the xenon lamp within 60 min (Fig. 5e). Moreover, the FL of the N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs showed consistent stability in different concentrations (0–2 mM) of NaCl solutions (Fig. 5f). The above results proved that the sensor had good stability, which provided the basis for the subsequent experiments.

The detection conditions were optimized to establish the ideal parameters for the experiments. The optimal excitation wavelengths for N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs were at 360 and 400 nm, respectively. Therefore, the emission spectra of N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs under excitation wavelengths ranging from 360 to 400 nm were investigated. At an excitation wavelength of 380 nm, both emission peaks of N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs were clearly distinguished and exhibited strong FL signals. Thus, 380 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength (Fig. 6a). The volume of N-Nb2C QDs was kept constant at 250 μL while gradually adding CQDs. With the addition of CQDs, the FL intensity of N-Nb2C QDs progressively quenched, whereas that of CQDs increased (Fig. 6b). Notably, when the volume of CQDs reached 150 μL, there was no further change in FL with the increase of the CQDs. Thus, the volume of N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs were set at 250 and 150 μL for experiments. Besides, I452/I544 showed minimal variation in the range of pH 4–9 except under extreme acids and alkalis (Fig. 6c). Considering the physiological environment relevant to real samples, pH = 7 was chosen as the optimal pH for analysis. Reaction time was analyzed by testing I452/I544 of N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs within 60 min after adding 50 μL FA (Fig. 6d). It was found that the I452/I544 remained stable after 1 min of reaction. All in all, an excitation of 380 nm, volumes of N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs were 250 and 150 μL, pH = 7, and a reaction time of 1 min constituted optimal experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor determination of FA
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the purchased FA is impure and produces the FL substance pterins under radiation, the FL spectra of FA (400 μM) under FL spectrometer radiation and exposure to natural light were investigated (Dántola et al., 2018; Eichholzer et al., 2006). The results showed that FA exhibited weak FL and remained stable for 60 min under the optimal detection conditions (Fig. S2a-b). Compared to the FL of the sensor, the FL of FA could be ignored. In addition, I452/I544 increased rapidly within 1 min and stabilized within 60 min when FA (400 μM) was added to the sensor, indicating a small error in the data obtained (Fig. S2c). Despite the purity of the FA employed being limited, the traces of other components present and the subsequent photochemical reactions exerted no significant effect on the FL spectra during testing. These results suggested that the sensor we constructed could be used as a reliable strategy to detect FA. The FL spectra of N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs upon the addition of different concentrations of FA were recorded. The introduction of FA quenched the FL of N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs. It was noteworthy that FA had a great effect on the FL at 544 nm and a small influence at 452 nm (Fig. 7a). Ratiometric detection was performed through the intensities of the N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs. The I452/I544 was calculated and demonstrated to have good linearity with FA in the concentration ranges of 0.1–100 μM and 100–400 μM, with linear equations expressed as y = 0.0033[C] + 0.5287 (R2 = 0.9976) and y = 0.0023[C] + 0.6434 (R2 = 0.9987), respectively, where C indicated the concentration of FA (Fig. 7b-c). It could be observed that the confidence bands contained all the data, indicating the data were reliable. The LOD was calculated as 87 nM according to the formula LOD = 3 s / k, where s was the standard deviation of the blank solution (n = 11) and k was the slope of the calibration curve. Additionally, recent works on fluorescent sensors for the detection of FA were summarized (Table S1). The comparison revealed that our work has a wider linear range, highlighting its potential application in practical analysis.

Considering the complexity of real samples, the effects of ions and small molecules that may coexist in real samples on the sensor were investigated to evaluate its selectivity and anti-interference ability. I452/I544 remained unaffected by multiple interfering substances, which indicated N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs had good selectivity toward FA (Fig. 8 ). Anti-interference experiments were performed by adding FA (50 μM) into the N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs in the presence of interfering substances (50 μM). The results revealed that N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs could still sensitively detect FA. Consequently, the ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs showed significant promise for practical sample detection due to its specificity and accuracy regarding FA.

Systematic experiments were conducted to analyze the FL quenching mechanism between FA and N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra obtained from adding FA to N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs were not significantly different from those theoretically calculated (Fig. 9a-b). This phenomenon represented that there was no static quenching mechanism (Liu et al., 2019). Furthermore, it was observed that the absorption spectra of FA partially overlapped with the FL spectra of both N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs (Fig. 9c-d). Therefore, it was hypothesized that the mechanism of FA quenching the FL of N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs might be the IFE or FRET (Zhang et al., 2021). Additionally, the FL lifetime of N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs did not change after mixing with FA, which demonstrated the presence of the IFE for both N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs with FA (Fig. 9e-f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor determination of FA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs for the detection of FA in real samples was demonstrated through the standard addition method. Oatmeal, orange juice, and urine were taken as real samples. Different concentrations of FA (10, 30, and 50 μM) were added to the above samples and the contents were calculated according to the linear equations. The ratiometric fluorescent sensor exhibited better recovery (95.64–106.15 %) and RSD ≤ 3.62 % (Table 1 ). This illustrated the high applicability of the sensor in real sample detection.

The advancement of smartphones and their increasingly diverse functional applications have created new opportunities for utilizing these devices in scientific research. Notably, the FL of the N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs changed from yellow to blue with increased concentrations of FA (0.1–350 μM), which promoted a strategy for the convenient identification of FA based on smartphone imaging (Fig. 10a-b). Calculations revealed that there was a good linear relationship between B/R and FA: y = 0.006[C] + 0.605 (R2 = 0.9844), and the LOD was determined as 189 nM (Fig. 10c). Moreover, the feasibility of this smartphone-assisted sensor was corroborated by theoretical analysis using CIE coordinates. The FL colors of N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs mixed with different concentrations of FA were similar to the CIE coordinates (Fig. 10d). This approach effectively circumvented the limitations associated with large instrument operations and prolonged time requirements, presenting promising prospects for on-site detection of FA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted sensor determination of FA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicality of the smartphone-assisted sensor, different concentrations of FA (10, 30, and 50 μM) were added to samples for testing. To verify the practicality of this strategy through the comparison of results, the samples selected for the two detection modes were consistent. The recoveries displayed by the sensor were ranged from 97.01 to 105.78 %, with RSD ≤ 4.08 % (Table S2).

By comparing the experimental results of the two modes, it was found that there was no significant difference between the two modes of data. This indicates that the results obtained by the dual-mode ratiometric fluorescent and smartphone-assisted sensor are reliable. This method not only has high accuracy in actual sample detection but also provides a convenient way to achieve visualization and real-time detection of FA.

In summary, a dual-mode ratiometric fluorescent and smartphone-assisted sensor was successfully developed for visual quantitative analysis of FA using N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs. N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs was established by mixing N-Nb2C QDs and CQDs. Based on the IFE, FA could rapidly quench the FL of the N-Nb2C QDs/CQDs and cause a significant change in the FL intensity ratio. Simultaneously, the FL color of the sensor changed gradually from yellow to blue. By combining color changes with smartphone technology, subtle visual variations were converted into quantifiable RGB values. Both modes exhibited high selectivity, accuracy (with recoveries >95 %), and compatibility with real samples. Therefore, this study not only opens new avenues for QDs in sensing and analysis but also offers a practical strategy for monitoring FA in food.

Lin Yuan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lizhen Liu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Investigation. Yunfeng Bai: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Yuzhen Wang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Lu Zhao: Investigation. Zhi Mi: Investigation. Jun Qin: Investigation. Feng Feng: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China (22476118) and Postgraduate Education Innovation Program of Shanxi Normal University (2024XBY07).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2025.143464.
